content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission.
June 3,
1993
David L.
Pen
PERI,
ALVARADO
&
FREY
Certified Public Accountants,
Inc.
P.O.
Box 22557
Santa Barbara,
CA
93121
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—218
Dear Mr. Pen:
This is in response to your letter dated May 25,
1993,
in
which you seek advice regarding the duties of Senator Gary K.
Hart
in regards to the expenditure of campaign funds under the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
QUESTION
May Senator Hart use campaign funds transferred from his
Senatorial campaign account to reimburse contributions made to his
now abandoned Superintendent of Public Instruction campaign?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts presented,
Senator Hart may use campaign
funds transferred from his Senatorial campaign account to
reimburse contributions made to his Superintendent of Public
Instruction campaign.
FACTS
Until recently,
Senator Hart was planning to run for
Superintendent of Public Instruction.
He opened a campaign
account and raised funds for this purpose.
He also transferred
campaign funds
from his existing Senatorial campaign account into
his new campaign account.
Recently,
Senator Hart decided not to run for Superintendent
of Public Instruction.
However, all of the campaign funds raised
for this purpose and all of the funds transferred into his new
committee from his Senatorial campaign account have been spent.
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless indicated otherwise.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322.5660


File No.
A—93—218
Page
2
Senator Hart desires to return the contributions made to his
Superintendent of Public Instruction campaign.
His committee has
sent letters to each of the contributors to the campaign asking
them to notify the committee if they desire to have their
contributions returned.
To date,
contributors have requested
return of $29,000 of the $42,665 contributed to the campaign.
Senator Hart has sufficient funds in his Senatorial campaign
account to return the contributions raised for the Superintendent
of Public Instruction campaign.
Senator Hart believes it is important to return the
contributions to his Superintendent of Public Instruction campaign
contributors in order to maintain his credibility and reputation
among his political supporters.
ANALYSIS
In Service Employees International Union, et al.
v. Fair
Political Practices Commission
(1992)
955 F.2d *1312, cert.
den.
112 S.Ct.
3056,
the court ruled that Section 85304 of the Act is
unconstitutional to the extent it prohibits a candidate from
transferring funds between his own campaign committees.
On this
basis,
Senator Hart is permitted to transfer campaign funds
between his Senatorial committee and Superintendent of Public
Instruction committee
(“Superintendent campaign.”).
Therefore,
the only issue is whether it is a proper expenditure of campaign
funds for Senator Hart to use the transferred funds to repay
contributions made to his Superintendent campaign.
No specific rules govern the return of non—surplus campaign
funds
2
.
We therefore look to Sections 89510,
89512
and 89513 of
the Act.
Section 89510(b)
states:
All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office.
.
. that he or she
intends to seek...
Section 89512 states:
An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful
execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510 if
it is reasonably related to a political purpose...
(Emphasis added.)
2
Compare Section 89519(b), whichrequires the pro rata return of
campaign funds to contributors in the event a candidate has left
office or has lost an election for which the funds were raised.


File No.
A—93—218
Page
3
Furthermore,
Section 89513(f) (1) prohibits the use of
campaign funds to make personal gifts unless the gift is directly
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.
However, this provision goes on to state:
11
The refund of a
campaign contribution does not constitute the making of a gift.”
Based on a reading of Sections 89510,
89512 and 89513, it is
clear that non—surplus campaign funds may be returned to
contributors so long as the return is reasonably related to a
political purpose.
Under the facts you present,
we believe the
use of these campaign funds to reimburse contributors to Senator
Hart’s Superintendent campaign is reasonably related to a
political purpose.
Senator Hart is an incumbent elected officer
who presumably has established a broad base of contributors during
the course of his various campaigns for public office.
Where he
asks those contributors for contributions to seek another office,
but thereafter changes his plans, it is reasonable for him to want
to return those funds to the contributors.
This is reasonably
related to his desire to maintain his credibility and reputation
among his political supporters.
3
Should you have any questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincere y,
S ot
Hallabrin
Assistant General Counsel
This is particularly so where,
as here,
the candidate offers a
return of the full amount of each contribution.
Whether it is
appropriate for a candidate to return contributions only to
selected contributors must be analyzed on a case—by—case basis.
(Hiltachk Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-053.)

Instruction campaign.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Senator Hart use campaign funds transferred from his
Senatorial campaign account to reimburse contributions made to his
now abandoned Superintendent of Public Instruction campaign?

CONCLUSION: Under the facts presented,
Senator Hart may use campaign
funds transferred from his Senatorial campaign account to
reimburse contributions made to his Superintendent of Public
Instruction campaign.
